content.text: The behaviour of an agent is only as debuggable as the record it leaves behind.
Structured logging for agentic AI request lifecycles

means emitting machine-parseable, consistently-keyed log events for every stage of an agent run — from the inbound request through planning, tool calls, and model completions to the final response — so the whole lifecycle can be queried, correlated, and audited.
What it is

Structured logging replaces free-text log lines with typed, key-value events (usually JSON) that carry a stable schema. For an agentic request the lifecycle spans several distinct stages: request received, prompt assembled, model invoked, tool called, tool result returned, guardrail evaluated, and response emitted. Each stage becomes a structured event sharing a common correlation ID (the trace or session ID) so the events for one request can be stitched into a coherent timeline.

The schema defines a fixed set of fields — timestamp, level, session_id, span_id, stage, model, latency_ms, token counts, tool name, outcome — plus stage-specific fields. Crucially, the schema also defines which fields may contain user content and must therefore be redacted, keeping the logging layer compatible with privacy controls downstream.
Why it matters

Agent runs are non-deterministic and multi-step, so a failure is rarely explained by a single line. Diagnosing why an agent looped, chose the wrong tool, or produced a bad answer requires reconstructing the entire decision path, and that is only possible if every stage was logged with consistent keys. Free-text logs force engineers to write brittle regexes and cannot be aggregated; structured logs can be filtered, grouped, and charted directly. Structured lifecycle logs also underpin cost accounting (token usage per stage), latency attribution (which stage was slow), and compliance (a queryable record of what the agent did).
Benefits
Whole-request reconstruction: a single correlation ID retrieves every stage of one agent run in order.
Queryable aggregation: filter by stage, model, tool, or outcome without parsing free text.
Latency and cost attribution down to the individual stage that caused a regression.
A stable schema makes new dashboards and alerts cheap to build on existing fields.
Redaction boundaries defined in the schema keep sensitive content out of the wrong fields by design.
Consistent keys let logs, traces, and metrics be correlated by shared IDs.
Trade-offs and limitations
Structured logs are more verbose and costly to store than terse text lines, especially at high request volume.
A schema is a contract: changing field names or types can break downstream dashboards and parsers.
Over-logging every intermediate token or full prompt inflates volume and raises privacy risk.
Developers must be disciplined about using the schema rather than falling back to ad-hoc fields.
Correlation depends on the ID being propagated through every async hop, which is easy to drop.
How it works

A logging library is configured to emit JSON with a fixed base schema and to inject the current correlation context automatically. At the start of a request a session ID and root span ID are established and stored in a context variable; every subsequent log call reads that context so events are automatically correlated without the developer passing IDs manually. Each lifecycle stage emits a start and end event, and the end event carries the stage's latency and outcome. A field-level redaction policy runs before serialisation so any field marked as user-content is masked or hashed.

The volume of structured logs per request grows with the number of stages, so sampling is applied at the request level: a configurable fraction of successful requests are logged in full, while all failed or flagged requests are logged in full regardless. This keeps cost bounded while preserving complete detail for the requests that matter.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Structured events emitted at each stage of an agent request, correlated by session ID

content.text: Each stage of the request emits structured events that share the session and span IDs. Events flow through a redaction step, then to a log shipper that forwards them to the log backend. Because every event carries the correlation ID and a stage field, the backend can reconstruct any single request's full timeline and aggregate across requests by any field.
When to use it
Any production agent system where debugging requires understanding multi-step behaviour.
Systems that must attribute cost or latency to specific stages of the agent run.
When logs feed compliance, audit, or quality-evaluation pipelines that need queryable fields.
Lower-value for a trivial single-shot completion with no tools — a single structured event suffices there.
How to implement
Define the log schema: base fields shared by all events plus per-stage fields, and mark content fields for redaction.
Adopt a structured logging library and configure JSON output with automatic context injection.
Establish a correlation context (session_id, span_id) at request entry and propagate it through async hops.
Emit start/end events for each lifecycle stage with latency and outcome on the end event.
Apply field-level redaction before serialisation for any field marked as user content.
Sample full logging for successful requests but always log failed or flagged requests in full.
Ship events to a backend that indexes the correlation ID and stage fields for fast retrieval.
Examples
Real-world example

A coding-assistant agent handling millions of requests a day emits structured events for six lifecycle stages per request. When users reported occasional 30-second stalls, engineers filtered logs to failed requests and grouped end events by stage. The latency was concentrated entirely in the tool_call stage for one specific tool — a code-search backend — which was timing out and being retried three times. Because each retry was a distinct structured event sharing the session ID, the retry storm was obvious in a single grouped query.

Full logging was sampled at 5% of successful requests but 100% of failures, so the stalled requests were all captured in full while storage stayed bounded. The fix — a shorter tool timeout with a single retry — was validated by watching the tool_call end-event latency distribution return to normal in the same dashboard.
Common mistakes
Logging full prompts and completions in plain fields, creating a privacy liability in the log store.
Using inconsistent field names across services so the same concept cannot be queried uniformly.
Dropping the correlation ID across an async boundary, breaking request reconstruction.
Emitting only an end event, losing the ability to detect stages that started but never finished.
No sampling, so structured-log volume and cost grow unbounded with traffic.
Best practices
Treat the schema as a versioned contract; add fields additively and avoid renaming.
Inject correlation context automatically so developers cannot forget to include it.
Emit paired start/end events per stage to detect hangs and attribute latency.
Define redaction at the schema level, not per log call, so content cannot leak by omission.
Sample successful requests but always fully log failures and guardrail-flagged runs.
Performance and scalability

Structured logging adds serialization cost per event; at high stage counts this is non-trivial. Emit at an appropriate level and serialize lazily so that debug-level fields are not built when the level is disabled. Volume is the dominant scaling factor: a request with six stages and start/end events produces twelve log lines, so a service at 1,000 requests per second emits 12,000 lines per second before sampling. Request-level sampling (log a fraction of successes in full, all failures) keeps ingestion cost roughly linear in the failure rate plus a small constant. Index only the low-cardinality fields (stage, outcome, model) heavily; treat content fields as unindexed to control index size.

Security and reliability

Because lifecycle logs can capture prompts, tool arguments, and completions, the redaction boundary is a security control, not a convenience. Enforce it in a shared serialization layer so no code path can bypass it, and test it with fixtures that assert content fields are masked. Reliability of the logging path must not compromise the request: use non-blocking, buffered shipping so a log-backend outage degrades observability rather than failing user requests. Cap in-memory buffers and drop-with-a-counter under back-pressure rather than growing unbounded.
Field-level redaction policy

The redaction policy is the bridge between structured logging and the privacy pipeline. Rather than deciding per call site what to mask, the schema tags each field with a sensitivity class — public, internal, or user-content — and the serializer applies a class-appropriate transform: pass-through, hash, or full mask. This makes redaction a property of the field name, so a developer who logs a sensitive value into a correctly-named field gets protection automatically, and one who invents an ad-hoc field is caught by a schema linter.

attrs.alt: Three layers of the structured log schema, with content fields redacted by class

content.text: Defaulting unknown fields to the strictest class means a newly-added field is protected until someone deliberately classifies it, which fails safe rather than leaking.
Propagating correlation across async and service boundaries

The single most common way lifecycle logging breaks is a dropped correlation ID at an async or network boundary. When an agent hands work to a background task, a queue, or a downstream microservice, the context variable holding the session and span IDs does not automatically travel with it. The fix is to serialise the correlation context into the message or request headers at the boundary and restore it on the other side, so a request that fans out across services still reconstructs as one timeline.

For HTTP calls this means injecting the trace context into request headers using the W3C traceparent format; for message queues it means placing the same fields into message attributes. On the receiving side, middleware reads the context back into the context variable before any handler runs, so downstream log calls are correlated without the handler doing anything.

# Propagate correlation context across a queue boundary
import json

def enqueue(task_body: dict):
    ctx = _ctx.get()
    envelope = {
        "traceparent": f"00-{ctx['session_id']}-{new_span_id()}-01",
        "body": task_body,
    }
    queue.put(json.dumps(envelope))

def dequeue_and_run(handler):
    envelope = json.loads(queue.get())
    tp = envelope["traceparent"].split("-")
    _ctx.set({"session_id": tp[1], "parent_span": tp[2]})
    log.info("task_received", extra={"stage": "async_task"})
    handler(envelope["body"])

# HTTP: inject on the client, restore in server middleware
def inject_headers(headers: dict) -> dict:
    ctx = _ctx.get()
    headers["traceparent"] = f"00-{ctx['session_id']}-{new_span_id()}-01"
    return headers

def restore_from_headers(headers: dict):
    tp = headers.get("traceparent", "").split("-")
    if len(tp) == 4:
        _ctx.set({"session_id": tp[1], "parent_span": tp[2]})
Serialise correlation context into headers (HTTP) or message attributes (queues) at every boundary.
Restore context in receiving middleware before any handler runs, so correlation is automatic downstream.
Use the W3C traceparent format so logs align with distributed traces from the same IDs.
Add a boundary-crossing test that asserts a fanned-out request reconstructs as a single session.
Emit a metric for events missing a session_id — a nonzero rate signals a dropped-context bug.
Never reconstruct correlation from timestamps; only an explicit propagated ID is reliable.

A small investment in boundary propagation pays off disproportionately: without it, exactly the multi-service requests that are hardest to debug are also the ones whose logs cannot be stitched together.
Key takeaways
Emit typed, consistently-keyed events for every stage of the agent request lifecycle.
Correlate all events by a session/span ID injected automatically from context.
Pair start and end events per stage to attribute latency and detect hangs.
Define redaction by field sensitivity class in a shared serializer that cannot be bypassed.
Sample successful requests in full but always fully log failures and flagged runs.
Index low-cardinality fields; keep content fields unindexed and masked to control cost and risk.
